dial_default:
One dial, one whole day
DOMY Watch shows all 24 hours on a single face: noon stands at the top, midnight at the bottom, evening on the right, morning on the left. The small hand makes ONE turn per day, the large hand one per hour — and the lit part of the dial IS your daylight, growing and shrinking with the seasons exactly as your sky does. The Earth rides the outer ring carrying today's date, the Moon rides its own hour wearing its true phase, and each weekday keeps its planet in its own diamond.
pointer_Prism:
The Prism
Six diamonds split the day into six moods, like white light through a prism: yellow noon Joy, orange afternoon Zeal, red evening Passion, purple midnight Sorrow, blue pre-dawn Calm, green morning Renewal. The star's top tip always points at TRUE solar noon for your exact location — watch it lean east or west of 12 through the year.
pointer_Seasons:
The Seasons
Four arms on the cross of the year — the solstices and equinoxes. Each arm wears its season's color and carries its temperament: the choleric summer at the top, the sanguine spring, the melancholic autumn, the phlegmatic winter. The plainest of the pointers, and the oldest reading of a day.
pointer_Compass:
The Compass
Eight points, like a wind rose laid over the hours: the noon arm, the working afternoon, sunset, the deep night and the dawn each get their own direction. The bottom arm gives up its diamond to the info slot, so the octa dial reads time and date in the same glance.
pointer_Trinity:
The Trinity
Three great diamonds divide the day into three watches — the morning, the afternoon and the night. The largest figures on any pointer live here; when the archetypes are on, the Trinity carries them at full size.
pointer_Aurora:
The Aurora wheel
Seven bands of northern light instead of arms — the day flows through them without a single straight line. No diamonds, no star: just the colors of the sky bending around the hours, with the weekday body riding its own stem.
pointer_Calendar:
The Calendar wheel
Twelve wedges, one per month, with the current month lit at the top by the hour. The year becomes the dial itself: you read the season from where the light falls, and the months turn under the hands like a second, slower clock.
archetype_prism:
The lancet windows
Switch the Archetype on and the Prism's six diamonds become six stained-glass lancets — the Persons of the canon, one per mood of the day. The window whose hour contains true solar noon burns lit while the others wait in shadow; the center rosette binds them into one wheel.
archetype_reveal:
The Omega reveal
Double-click the Omega letter at the bottom of the ring and the hands step aside for one minute: every figure comes to full light with its name beneath it — The One at noon, Jesus and Michael on the day arms, Judas, Lucifer and The Devil in the night. A second double-click ends the audience early.
earth_card:
The Earth card
Rest the cursor on the Earth marker and the date speaks: the full date with its day and week ordinals, the era emblem with its Anno Lucis year, the season badge with the day's count, and the zodiac sign with its dates. During an eclipse the card grows the eclipse line and its emblem.
tetramorph_hover:
The Tetramorph
On the Seasons' light roster the four living creatures stand watch — Lion, Ox, Eagle and Man on their fixed-cross arms. Hovering one opens the three-column card: the creature, the Evangelist it became, and the element it holds. The rondels rotate daily between their two painted generations.
metal_gold:
Gold
Every metal theme can be struck in three finishes, recolored live from one master — no separate art. Gold is the warm default: the medallions read like struck coins in afternoon light.
metal_bronze:
Bronze
The same theme in bronze — darker, older, closer to a museum case. The recolor touches only the metal; the drawing beneath never changes.
metal_silver:
Silver
And in silver — the cold moonlit strike. Pick the finish per theme, or let every theme follow the ring's own metal with one switch.
mason_g_motto:
The Mason ring
The ring of the Great Seal: G at noon, Omega at midnight, and the two mottos arcing outside the band exactly as the Seal carries them — ANNUIT COEPTIS split around the crown with one long breath over the G, NOVUS ORDO SECLORUM riding the bottom coin-upright. Two metals by default: gold for the word letters, silver for the crown pair.
eclipse_solar:
A solar eclipse
When the catalog holds a solar eclipse near the traveled moment, the Earth marker wears the black eclipsed-Sun plate and its glow burns red at the eclipse hour — total burns full, annular wears its ring of fire, partial scales with magnitude. The corona is drawn as light, not as a picture.
eclipse_lunar:
A lunar eclipse
A total lunar eclipse turns the Moon's disc itself copper — a true darkening, not a wash — with the thin turquoise ozone fringe at the rim, the same light that real eclipse photographs catch. Partial and penumbral eclipses darken the disc by their own lesser degrees.
time_travel:
Time Travel
Any moment, any place: set a date across nearly thirty thousand years (or two hundred thousand with the full installation), move the observer anywhere on Earth, and the dial shows that sky for sixty seconds before returning to now. The header answers with the era line — 14 July 1789 is the 5868th year of Light.
quick_jump:
Quick Jump
One-click journeys under the tray and dial menu: the next solstice or equinox by the Sun, the next phase or eclipse by the Moon, a year or a century forward or back — and under Location, the two poles with their polar day and night, and Greenwich on the meridian. Jumps chain: each starts from the moment you are already visiting.
observatory_full:
The Observatory
Five instruments in one window: season durations across thirty millennia, the light-minus-dark envelope with the Age of Light shaded and every real peak labeled, the eclipse timeline around the present, the day-length curve of your own latitude's year, and the Laskar long envelope reaching two hundred thousand years each way.
observatory_zoom:
Zoom
Every chart zooms under the wheel, centered on the cursor, down to single years — the tick ladder adapts as you dive, from ten-millennia steps to year marks. The Laskar chart keeps its honesty caption: beyond the measured window the amplitude is trend, not date.
observatory_enlarge:
Enlarge
Any instrument opens alone in its own window at full size — here the Laskar envelope, with the measured window shaded around the present and the eccentricity minimum marked ahead. The legend reads the exact values under the cursor.
encyclopedia_gallery:
The Encyclopedia
Every symbol on the dial has a written home: the gallery gathers them by family — the Clock itself, the Gods of the week across seven mythologies, the Wider Pantheon, the Zodiac, the eclipses, the eras and the archetypes. Press SPACE over almost anything on the dial to jump straight to its page.
encyclopedia_eclipse:
The eclipse chapters
Each eclipse family keeps its own chapters — what happens in the sky, what you will see, how the dial wears it, how often it returns, and the anchor story from history. The overview pages carry the category emblems; Herodotus' battle-stopping eclipse of 585 BCE opens the solar line.
moon_phases_page:
The Moon pages
Eight phases, each with its own page — and the phase image is drawn LIVE by the same terminator geometry that shades the dial's Moon, never a stored picture. The Sky tells the astronomy, the Story tells the old readings, and On the Dial ties both back to what you see.
settings_nav:
Settings
Everything tunable lives in one dialog with a navigation column: Location for your observer, Display for the dial's furniture, Colors for palettes, clock tint and saturation, Custom art for your own images, Themes, Language and System. The dial applies changes the moment you confirm.
